g.data-narration: Welcome to Linked Lists - the foundation of dynamic data structures.
g.text: Understanding the fundamental dynamic data structure

g.data-narration: A linked list stores data in nodes connected by pointers, unlike arrays that store contiguously.
Each node contains data and a pointer to the next node, creating the chain structure.
Arrays provide O(1) random access but O(n) insertions, while linked lists reverse this tradeoff.
g.text: Array vs Linked List - Key Differences

g.data-narration: There are four main types: singly, doubly, circular, and circular doubly - each with different pointer configurations.
Understanding time complexity helps choose the right data structure for your specific use case.
Here's a basic C implementation showing how to create nodes and traverse the linked list.
Each node is allocated separately on the heap, connected through pointers stored on the stack.
Linked lists excel at insertions but suffer from poor random access and cache performance compared to arrays.
Linked lists power everyday applications like music playlists, browser history, and undo functionality in editors.
Key takeaways: Linked lists trade random access for efficient insertions. Always maintain the head pointer and handle edge cases carefully.
